input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.reasoningText: The user wants me to benchmark three methods (AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, UniqueWordCount) from TextAnalyzer.cs to compare .NET 9 vs .NET 10 performance. Let me start by exploring the codebase and checking the available .NET SDKs.

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "dotnet --list-sdks && echo \"---\" && dotnet --list-runtimes", "description": "Check available .NET SDKs and runtimes"}

data.content: Good — both .NET 9 and .NET 10 SDKs and runtimes are available. Let me read the source files.
Now I'll create a BenchmarkDotNet project that multi-targets net9.0 and net10.0.
data.reasoningText: Now I need to create a BenchmarkDotNet project that targets both net9.0 and net10.0 to benchmark those three methods. Let me create the benchmark project.

toolCalls.name: create

toolResult.sessionLog:
diff --git a/tmp/sv-77e5e9fbfd7f4125a7b227eb96659e30/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj b/tmp/sv-77e5e9fbfd7f4125a7b227eb96659e30/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-77e5e9fbfd7f4125a7b227eb96659e30/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
@@ -1,0 +1,15 @@
+<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.*" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <Compile Include="../TextAnalyzer/TextAnalyzer.cs" Link="TextAnalyzer.cs" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet doesn't recognize .NET 10 yet. I'll write a manual benchmark instead.
data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet's SDK validator doesn't recognize .NET 10 yet. I need a different approach. Let me try a newer version of BenchmarkDotNet, or maybe I should just write a simple manual benchmark instead since BenchmarkDotNet is having issues with .NET 10 support.

Actually, let me check what version of BenchmarkDotNet is being installed. Maybe there's a nightly/prerelease that supports .NET 10. Or I could just write a manual benchmark that does its own timing with Stopwatch, running each method many times, and compare. That would be simpler and avoid BenchmarkDotNet compatibility issues.


I'll go with a manual benchmark approach instead—it'll be straightforward and avoid the compatibility headaches.
